attendees.note: No response to invite sent Jan 22 and Jan 27

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. Kevin and Tom aren't on — I'll follow up with Kevin after. Priya, thanks for joining. Marcus, where are we?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Honestly, not great. We sent our review comments back to DataBridge on January 20th with the revised mapping requirements. We haven't heard anything back. Tom's been our contact and he's been — radio silent.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: When you say revised requirements — what changed?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: So the v1 mapping Tom sent on the 15th had about twelve issues that Linda and I flagged. Mostly around the ADT feed mappings and the custom lab extensions. We documented everything in a comment log and sent it back with a revised version of the mapping document — we call it v1.1. We asked for confirmation that Tom received it and a revised timeline for his team to incorporate the changes. Nothing.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. So the mapping is not approved.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: No. And until DataBridge comes back to us on our comments, we can't give approval.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I need to flag something. CloudPeak has been operating on the assumption that the mapping was approved and that we'd have the architecture package from DataBridge by the 29th — which is tomorrow. Jordan has already provisioned the base compute environment. If the mapping isn't approved and the architecture isn't coming tomorrow, that's a problem for us.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: What kind of problem? Can you hold the environment?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: We can hold it for a couple of weeks, but there are costs. Also, the security group configuration Jordan set up is based on the single-phase architecture that Tom described — I got a note from Tom about the phasing change back in January but we never got the full architecture doc, so Jordan built on what he knew from the kickoff plus Tom's email. If the architecture changes again, we'd need to redo that work.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Wait — you got a note from Tom about the phasing change?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Yeah, I got a brief email — let me find it — it said the team had decided to go single-phase and the architecture doc would reflect that. But I never got the actual architecture doc.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. So you know about the single-phase decision. Good. But Jordan built on incomplete architecture info.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Correct. We made some assumptions. We need that architecture doc to validate our configuration before we can say the environment is ready.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah, I have to be honest — I'm worried. Tom going silent doesn't feel like 'we're heads down building.' It feels like something's wrong on their end.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I know. I've sent two calendar invites to Kevin and nothing. I'm going to try calling Kevin after this call.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I actually... I was looking at LinkedIn last night and I think Tom Reyes may have changed his job. His profile says he started somewhere new in January.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: You're kidding.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'm not. I haven't confirmed it. But if Tom left DataBridge and nobody told us, that would explain a lot.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. Let me reach out to Kevin directly — not just calendar invites, I'll call. And I'll email Aisha, she's been on the calls. If Tom is gone, Aisha may be the best way in.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I want to make sure I understand the current state. The data mapping is not approved. DataBridge has not responded to Acme's review comments. The architecture package was due tomorrow and it doesn't sound like it's coming. Is that right?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's right. And the February 14th cutover is looking very tight.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Sarah, I have to be direct — if we don't have the architecture from DataBridge by January 30th at the latest, we cannot deliver a validated environment by February 14th. That's a hard constraint. Our SLA with you is ten business days of configuration time after we have all artifacts.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I hear you. I'm going to get DataBridge on the phone today. Marcus, if you don't hear from me by end of day, send me a message.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And Sarah — Diana should probably know about this. The cutover date is at risk and she set that date for a reason.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yeah. Let me try to get DataBridge back on line first. I don't want to escalate if this is just a communication glitch. Once I know what we're dealing with I'll decide whether to loop in Diana.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Understood. But the clock is ticking.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Sarah, while we're waiting to see what DataBridge says — can I walk through what CloudPeak's exposure is right now? I want to make sure everyone understands the situation.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Please.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Jordan has provisioned the base compute environment — VPC, subnets, IAM roles, the Amazon MQ broker configuration, and the Fargate cluster. He started from the kickoff architecture Tom described and incorporated the single-phase change from Tom's email. But because we never got the architecture doc, there are about a dozen configuration parameters Jordan estimated rather than confirmed. Security group rules, task sizing, API gateway config.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: How much rework is that if the architecture doc changes those parameters?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Some of it is straightforward — security group rule updates are quick. The task sizing could require a re-deploy. The API gateway config depends on how far off our assumptions are. Best case, a day of work. Worst case, three or four days if Jordan's assumptions are significantly off.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. So there's risk but it's not catastrophic as long as we get the architecture doc soon.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Correct. The longer it takes, the more Jordan builds on assumptions that might be wrong, and the more rework we potentially face.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah, I want to share something. I reached out to Aisha directly last week — not through formal channels, just a quick message on Slack in the shared channel. She didn't respond there either. Her last message in the channel was January 21st.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: January 21st. So she went quiet the day before the mapping approval was due.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's right. And Tom's last message in the channel was January 19th. I can see when they were last active — they've both been offline in the shared workspace.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Something happened at DataBridge. This isn't a communication glitch.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: What's our contractual position if DataBridge is experiencing internal problems? Is there a notice of delay provision?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Kevin would know better than me. That's another reason I need to get him on the phone. The SOW has a delay clause but I don't know the specifics off the top of my head.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: If Tom really did leave DataBridge in January, that raises a question about the knowledge transfer. Tom was the primary architect. Aisha is the integration developer — does she have enough context to complete the build without him?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Aisha was on the technical review call and she seemed very capable. She was already building the test framework and had reviewed the mapping with Tom. My hope is that if Tom left, Aisha has enough to carry it — or that DataBridge assigns someone new.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: The other scenario is that Tom leaving created organizational chaos and the whole project got dropped in the shuffle. That would explain why nobody's responding.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's the worst case scenario. Let's not assume that yet.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: What about a call with Kevin's manager? If Kevin isn't responding either, maybe we need to go above him.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll try Kevin directly first — call and email. If I don't hear back by end of day, I'll look for his manager's contact. Kevin's email signature may have an escalation line.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Alternatively — Acme's procurement team may have a vendor contact record for DataBridge with more than just Kevin's name. Worth checking.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Good thought. I'll ask our vendor management team. They handled the contracting so they may have multiple contacts.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yes, please do that today Marcus. It gives us a backup if my call to Kevin doesn't land.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: I'll reach out to vendor management as soon as we're off this call.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Thank you. Now — let's also talk about what happens if we can't re-engage DataBridge. What are our options? I don't want to get to February 7th having done nothing but wait.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: From the CloudPeak side — we can continue building the environment based on our current assumptions. The risk is rework if the architecture changes. But if DataBridge does eventually deliver and their architecture is close to what Jordan assumed, we could be in reasonably good shape.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: What's the probability Jordan's assumptions are close enough?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Tom's email about the single-phase decision was actually fairly detailed. Jordan thinks his assumptions are probably 70-80% right. The unknowns are mostly in the network configuration and the API gateway routing rules.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. So Priya, my ask is that you and Jordan keep building on your current assumptions, document what you've assumed versus confirmed, and be ready to adjust when we get the architecture doc. Does that work?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Yes. Jordan is already doing that — he's flagging assumed parameters in the deployment templates with a comment tag. It'll be easy to find and update them.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. That's the CloudPeak path. Now for DataBridge's work — the transformation logic and the mapping — we're in a harder position because that's entirely inside their shop. We don't have access to their build environment.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: We have the v1 mapping doc Tom sent on the 15th. And we have our v1.1 with comments. Even if DataBridge is in chaos, we have the primary artifact. The question is whether someone can pick up the build from that document.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's an important point. If it came to it — and I'm not saying it will — could Acme engage a different vendor to do the build against the mapping doc we have?

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: In theory yes. The mapping doc is an Acme artifact — we co-developed it. But onboarding a new vendor in this timeline would be... very difficult.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. That's the nuclear option. I'm not going there yet. But it's worth knowing it's on the table if needed.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: For what it's worth — CloudPeak has a professional services team that does FHIR integration work. If Acme needed a second opinion or emergency support, that's an avenue. I'm not pitching it, just making sure you know it exists.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good to know. I'll keep that in the back pocket.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah, I want to make sure we document the steps we've already taken to contact DataBridge. If this ends up in a legal conversation, we need a clear record.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. I've been keeping a log. The key touchpoints: I sent Kevin calendar invites on January 22nd and January 27th — no response. I emailed Tom on January 23rd — no response. I emailed Aisha on January 26th — no response. Marcus sent a Slack message to Aisha — no response. Those are all documented.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: We submitted our review comments with the v1.1 mapping doc on January 20th. That email is also on record.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. I'll add that to the contact log. We're building a clear paper trail in case we need it.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: On our side — I have Tom's email from mid-January about the phasing change. Jordan has his own email thread with Tom from early January about the network architecture. Those are in our records.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Can you forward those to me? I want to centralize everything in case there's a vendor dispute.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I'll do that today.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah, let's talk about the February 14th date for a moment. If we don't have DataBridge back online by February 1st — Priya's hard deadline — is there any flexibility on the cutover date? You said Diana has a board-approved date, but surely they'd rather delay than go live on a broken integration.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: There's budget approval tied to it. But if we can't safely deliver, Diana would rather know now than be surprised on February 13th. I'll have that conversation with her if we don't get DataBridge back online this week.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: What's the next board meeting where a date extension would be approved?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I don't know. Diana would. But if the extension requires a board meeting that's weeks away, that's a serious problem.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Presumably there's an executive authority to approve a short extension without a full board meeting. Especially if it's framed as vendor performance issue, not a project failure.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's a conversation for Diana and legal. I'll raise it with her once I have a clearer picture of what DataBridge is going to do.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Okay. I just want to make sure we're not heading toward a situation where everyone assumes someone else is going to raise the bad news with the executive sponsor.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I will raise it. But I want to try to get DataBridge re-engaged today first. If I call Kevin and there's a simple explanation — someone was sick, email went to spam, whatever — I don't want to have put Diana in crisis mode unnecessarily. Give me until 5 PM today.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's fair.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Agreed. 5 PM today. If we haven't heard anything, I'd say the escalation to Diana becomes necessary.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Understood. I'll send both of you a quick Slack message this afternoon once I've tried Kevin.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And if you do get Kevin on the phone — what's the ask? What do we need from him?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Three things. One — an explanation for why there's been no communication. Two — confirmation that DataBridge is still on the engagement and who the current technical lead is. Three — a committed date for a revised delivery of the mapping response and the architecture package.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And if the revised dates put us past February 1st for the architecture?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Then we escalate to Diana immediately. That's the trigger.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: That's clear. I'm comfortable with that framework.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Me too. Sarah, I appreciate you handling the vendor side of this. I know it's an uncomfortable call to make.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: It's part of the job. I'd rather have the uncomfortable conversation now than in two weeks.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: While we're all here — Marcus, is there anything on the Acme side that has slipped or is at risk that we should be aware of? Not just DataBridge.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: The location crosswalk and the abnormal flag code table — those were supposed to go to DataBridge by January 20th. I sent them to Tom's email on January 20th but obviously he hasn't confirmed receipt. If a new technical lead steps in, they'll need those.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Good. Keep those docs ready to resend the moment we have a live contact at DataBridge.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: They're ready. Also — the BAA. Kevin said it would be ready by January 13th. Was it ever sent to us?

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Yes — that came through on time, actually. January 12th. Legal signed it on the 13th. So the BAA is in place.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Okay, good. At least that's not an open item.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: On the CloudPeak side — I want to flag that Jordan reached out to Dev Rajan at Acme IT two weeks ago. Dev has been responsive. The VPN configuration is actually in good shape — that's one area where we made real progress. The site-to-site VPN is established and tested.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's good news. So network connectivity is ready to go.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Correct. Jordan and Dev did the connectivity testing last week. MLLP port is accessible from the CloudPeak VPC. That piece is done.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: That's a bright spot. Let's make sure that's in the status update.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Also, the HAPI FHIR test server Jordan stood up — that's been running since January 20th. It's just sitting there waiting for DataBridge to connect their transformation service.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: So from CloudPeak's side, you're actually in a reasonable position. It's really DataBridge's build that's the blocker.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Correct. If DataBridge came back online tomorrow with an architecture doc, we could be validated within ten business days. The infrastructure is largely there.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's actually more hopeful than I thought. The environment is mostly ready.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: It is. The problem is the transformation logic and the approved mapping — both of those live inside DataBridge's deliverable. We can't substitute for that.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Understood. So the gap is specifically DataBridge's build — the mapping response and the transformation service. Everything else is either done or in progress.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: And our UAT. But we can't start UAT until we have a working integration service to test.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Right. UAT is downstream of DataBridge delivery.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: I want to make one more point for the record. CloudPeak's contract is a time-and-materials engagement for the infrastructure work. We bill for Jordan's time regardless of whether the project completes. If this drags, the costs mount on Acme's side. I'm not saying that to be difficult — just making sure the financial context is clear.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Noted. I'll make sure Diana understands the cost implications of a delay.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: That's another reason to escalate sooner rather than later.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Agreed. My 5 PM deadline stands, but if I can't get Kevin on the phone by noon I'll loop Diana in earlier.

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: That sounds right.

transcript.speaker: Marcus Webb
transcript.text: Sarah — keep us posted today. I'm in back-to-back meetings until 3 but I'll check Slack.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: I'll post in Slack the moment I have anything. Okay. I think we've covered everything we can cover right now. Let me do a quick action item summary.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Okay. Action items: I'm calling Kevin Park today and emailing Aisha. Marcus holds on any escalation to Diana until I have more info. Priya, if I can get DataBridge engaged today, what's the fastest path to getting the architecture to you?

transcript.speaker: Priya Nair
transcript.text: Tom or Aisha sends the architecture doc to me and Jordan, we'll start reviewing immediately. If we get it by January 30th we can still make February 14th work — barely.

transcript.speaker: Sarah Chen
transcript.text: Got it. January 30th is the drop-dead date for the architecture doc. I'll convey that.
